title: Linguine ai gamberi
subtitle: Linguine with prawns, garlic, chilli, and white wine
excerpt: Linguine with raw prawns tossed in garlic, chilli, white wine, and olive oil, the Italian coastline preparation at its simplest. The prawns cook in 3 minutes and the sauce takes even less. Ready before the pasta finishes boiling.
sourceNotes: Based on the Italian linguine ai gamberi tradition as documented in Giorgio Locatelli, 'Made in Italy' (2006) and Anna Del Conte, 'Gastronomy of Italy' (1987). The dish belongs to the broad family of aglio-olio-e-peperoncino preparations extended with shellfish.

content.text: Linguine ai gamberi is the kind of dish that reveals how quickly good pasta can be made. The sauce is five ingredients plus pasta water, the prawns take three minutes, and the whole assembly takes about the same time as the pasta boiling. The key is using raw prawns rather than pre-cooked: raw prawns absorb the garlic and white wine flavours as they cook, whereas pre-cooked prawns turn rubbery and taste of nothing.

No cheese. Cheese with seafood is one of the hard rules of Italian coastal cooking. It overwhelms the prawn flavour rather than supporting it. Parsley provides freshness, lemon gives acidity, and a generous pour of good olive oil finishes the dish.

items.name: Raw king prawns
items.prepNote: peeled and deveined; shell-on for more flavour if preferred

content.text: Cook the pasta and make the sauce simultaneously

Start {{linguine}} of linguine in a large pot of well-salted boiling water. While it cooks, heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over a medium-high heat. Add {{garlic}} sliced garlic and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli. When the garlic begins to colour at the edges, add {{raw-king-prawns}} of raw prawns in a single layer. Cook for 90 seconds per side until pink and curled.

Pour {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine into the prawn pan and let it bubble for 1 minute. Reserve a mug of pasta water from the boiling pasta, then drain. Add the pasta to the prawn pan over a medium heat, tossing to combine with a splash of pasta water. Squeeze {{lemon}} of lemon juice over and scatter {{parsley}} of chopped parsley. Toss once more and serve immediately.

items.fix: Raw prawns take 90 seconds per side at high heat. They are done when they turn pink and curl into a C shape. An O shape means overcooked.
Add pasta water a tablespoon at a time when tossing. The sauce should coat the pasta lightly without pooling.
items.cause: Wine reduced too far, or pasta water not added

items.fix: The garlic should sizzle gently and colour slowly. If it browns within 30 seconds of going in, the heat is too high.
items.symptom: Garlic burnt before prawns are added

content.text: Pasta with shellfish in the aglio-olio-peperoncino style is the classic dish of the Italian coast from Campania to Sicily. The base of garlic, olive oil, chilli, and white wine suits nearly every shellfish. It works for clams, mussels, prawns, and scallops with little change to the method. Linguine ai gamberi is the prawn version. It is one of the most ordered pasta dishes in Italian seaside restaurants.
